Welcome back. At this point you've learned how to conduct yourself in an initial meeting with a seller. We left off with you parting the meeting, framing the next steps as signing a non-disclosure agreement and exchanging more detailed and proprietary information with the confidence it will stay private. On a non-market deal, your professional will have already sent the NDA. In an off-market deal, within an hour of the first meeting, you send your NDA. That way you show that you're credible and that you're interested in continuing the conversation. Now, NDAs need to be signed by both disclosing parties. There's a small chance that either side may want to redline or create edits to the NDA you sent. Don't worry about it. Almost every NDA I've ever since had a few comments, so that's not a big issue. If he or she does, you'll need to judge how nitpicky they're being. Ask questions in the case like, what are you trying to accomplish with this edit? If the edits are too cumbersome or the seller's reason is too strange, it's an important signal of what's to come in the due diligence and negotiation phase. It may signal that despite your initial meeting where the stakes were lower, the seller has a very difficult time being able to work through the process of potentially selling their business or they're going to be so hard to deal with that you'll never be able to get a deal done. If you get the sense this potential seller might be hard to work with, it might be a good signal to move on. Some people have the tendency to just complicate all matters and then you spend a lot of time, energy and effort wasting your time. But once the NDA is signed, you're going to start asking for more information. Depending on the size of the business and how well managed they are, the information they provide could tell you a lot or not enough. This is the last green or red light you're looking for in order to proceed with in-depth due diligence and negotiation process. Here's what you'll need to see in this round. These are things that could materially impact your decision to proceed, such as the largest customer's contract is coming up and they intend to not renew. You should know that. So have the customer names in a number format and then have the details of the customer to the side of it so they're not disclosing the name of their customers to you.
